title: Chapter 1. Condensation
content:
The greenhouse was brightest in winter. Mara had always thought that unfair. Summer made flowers of itself without assistance, but winter demanded architecture, panes, angles, and patient hands.

Each morning she unlocked the glass house before sunrise and walked its length with a cloth over one shoulder, wiping condensation from the interior frames. The droplets caught the first gray light and held it long enough to make the room feel inhabited before anyone arrived.

On the third Monday in January she found an envelope balanced on the bench beside the citrus trays.

It had no stamp. No address. Only her name, written in a dark blue ink that pressed a little too hard at the downstrokes.

Mara,

The camellia by the south wall has opened three days too early. I thought you would want a witness.

There was no signature, yet she knew at once who had written it. Only one person in the entire estate treated bloom times like confidences rather than facts.

She folded the note again, slower than necessary, and looked toward the far corner where winter roses climbed the inner trellis. The glass there was misted white. No figure waited beyond it. Still, the room had the unmistakable feeling of having just been left.
